question: What are the four pillars of Object-Oriented Programming? Explain each briefly.
idealAnswer: 1) Encapsulation: Bundling data and methods that operate on that data within a class, hiding internal details. Access modifiers (private, protected, public) control visibility. 2) Inheritance: A class (child) can inherit properties and methods from another class (parent). Enables code reuse. 'is-a' relationship. 3) Polymorphism: Same interface, different implementations. Compile-time (function overloading) and runtime (virtual functions/method overriding). 4) Abstraction: Hiding complex implementation details and showing only the necessary interface. Achieved via abstract classes and interfaces.

question: Explain the difference between 'is-a' and 'has-a' relationships in OOP with examples.
idealAnswer: 'is-a' represents inheritance — a child class IS A type of parent class. Example: Dog is-a Animal, Car is-a Vehicle. Implemented with class Dog : public Animal. 'has-a' represents composition — a class HAS A member of another class. Example: Car has-a Engine, University has-a Department. Implemented by having an Engine object as a member of Car class. Prefer composition over inheritance when possible because it provides better flexibility, loose coupling, and avoids the fragile base class problem. Inheritance creates tight coupling.

question: Write a C++ example demonstrating runtime polymorphism using virtual functions.
hints: Use a base class pointer to call derived class methods
keyPoints: base class pointer calls derived method

question: What is the difference between an abstract class and an interface? When would you use each?
idealAnswer: Abstract class: Can have both abstract (pure virtual) and concrete methods. Can have member variables and constructors. Supports access modifiers. A class can inherit only one abstract class (in C++/Java). Use when: classes share common behavior AND state. Interface: Only method signatures (pure virtual in C++, interface in Java). No member variables (except constants). All methods are implicitly public. A class can implement multiple interfaces. Use when: unrelated classes need to follow the same contract. Example: Animal abstract class (has name, age + abstract makeSound()). Printable interface (has print() method — any class can be Printable).
keyPoints: abstract class can have concrete methods

question: What is encapsulation? How is it implemented in C++? Why is it important?
idealAnswer: Encapsulation bundles data (member variables) and the methods that operate on that data into a single unit (class), restricting direct access to internal data. Implemented using access modifiers: private (accessible only within class), protected (within class and derived classes), public (accessible everywhere). Getters and setters provide controlled access to private data. Importance: 1) Data protection — prevents invalid states. 2) Implementation hiding — can change internals without affecting users. 3) Maintainability — changes are localized. 4) Validation — setters can validate input before setting.

question: Explain constructors and destructors in C++. What are the different types of constructors?
idealAnswer: Constructor: Special method called automatically when an object is created. Same name as class, no return type. Types: 1) Default constructor — no parameters: MyClass() {}. 2) Parameterized constructor — takes arguments: MyClass(int x) { this->x = x; }. 3) Copy constructor — creates object from another: MyClass(const MyClass& other). Called when passing by value or initializing from another object. Destructor: Called automatically when object goes out of scope or is deleted. Name: ~MyClass(). Used for cleanup (freeing memory, closing files). No parameters, no return type, only one per class. Order: Constructors called in creation order, destructors in reverse order.

question: Explain the SOLID principles in OOP. You can briefly describe each.
idealAnswer: S — Single Responsibility: A class should have only one reason to change. Example: Don't mix database logic and UI logic in one class. O — Open/Closed: Classes should be open for extension but closed for modification. Use inheritance/interfaces to add behavior without changing existing code. L — Liskov Substitution: Derived classes should be substitutable for their base classes without breaking functionality. If Square extends Rectangle, it should work wherever Rectangle is used. I — Interface Segregation: Don't force classes to implement interfaces they don't use. Many specific interfaces are better than one general-purpose interface. D — Dependency Inversion: High-level modules should depend on abstractions, not concrete implementations. Use interfaces for dependencies.

hints: Constructor order: parent first. Destructor order: child first.
idealAnswer:
Output:
Base Constructor
Derived Constructor
Derived Destructor
Base Destructor

Constructors are called from base to derived (parent first, then child). Destructors are called in reverse order (child first, then parent). This ensures the base part is fully constructed before the derived part uses it, and the derived part is cleaned up before the base part is destroyed.
keyPoints: output is Base Derived Derived Base

question: What is the difference between function overloading and function overriding in C++?
idealAnswer: Function Overloading (Compile-time polymorphism): Same function name but different parameter lists (number, type, or order). Resolved at compile time by the compiler. Can happen in the same class. Example: int add(int a, int b) and float add(float a, float b). Function Overriding (Runtime polymorphism): Derived class provides a specific implementation of a method already defined in the base class. Same name, same parameters, same return type. Requires virtual keyword in base class. Resolved at runtime via vtable. Must happen across inheritance hierarchy. Example: Base::draw() overridden by Derived::draw().
keyPoints: overriding = same signature in derived class

question: Explain the Singleton design pattern. When would you use it? What are its drawbacks?
What is the 'static' keyword in C++? Explain static variables and static methods.
idealAnswer: Static Variable: Inside a function — retains value between calls (not destroyed on function return). Inside a class — shared by all objects (one copy for all instances). Exists even without creating an object. Static Method: Belongs to the class, not to any object. Can be called using ClassName::method(). Can only access static members (not instance variables). Cannot use 'this' pointer. Use case: Utility functions, counters shared across instances, factory methods. Example: Math::sqrt(), a counter tracking total objects created.
keyPoints: static var shared by all objects
use for utilities and shared data

question: Compare procedural programming and object-oriented programming. Give advantages of OOP.
idealAnswer: Procedural: Program is a sequence of functions operating on data. Data and functions are separate. Top-down approach. Examples: C, Pascal. OOP: Program is a collection of objects that contain both data and methods. Bottom-up approach. Examples: C++, Java, Python. Advantages of OOP: 1) Modularity — code organized into classes. 2) Reusability — inheritance enables code reuse. 3) Maintainability — encapsulation localizes changes. 4) Flexibility — polymorphism allows different behaviors. 5) Real-world modeling — objects map to real entities. Disadvantages of OOP: Steeper learning curve, can be overkill for simple programs, potential overhead.

question: Write a C++ abstract class 'Vehicle' with pure virtual functions start() and stop(). Create a 'Car' class that inherits from it and implements these functions.
keyPoints: derived must implement all pure virtual

question: In C++, class A has a private member x, protected member y, and public member z. Class B inherits publicly from A. What can B access? What can an object of B access?
idealAnswer:
Inside class B (member functions): Can access y (protected) and z (public) from A. CANNOT access x (private) — private members are never accessible by derived classes. Outside (object of B): Can only access z (public). Cannot access x (private) or y (protected) — protected is accessible within the class hierarchy only, not from outside. Summary table:
- Private x: A only
- Protected y: A and B (inside), not outside
- Public z: A, B, and outside
Protected inheritance would make y and z protected in B. Private inheritance would make both private in B.

question: What is a virtual destructor? Why is it important when using polymorphism?
hints: What happens when you delete a derived object through a base pointer?
idealAnswer: A virtual destructor ensures that when a derived class object is deleted through a base class pointer, the derived class destructor is called first, followed by the base class destructor. Without virtual destructor: Base* p = new Derived(); delete p; — only Base's destructor runs, Derived's destructor is skipped. This causes resource leaks if Derived allocated memory or opened files. With virtual destructor: Both destructors run correctly (Derived first, then Base). Rule: If a class has ANY virtual function, it should have a virtual destructor. The destructor is virtual to ensure proper cleanup through polymorphic pointers.

question: What are templates in C++? Show a simple example of a function template.
Explain the Factory Method design pattern briefly. Give a use case.
idealAnswer: Factory Method defines an interface for creating objects but lets subclasses decide which class to instantiate. Instead of calling 'new ClassName()' directly, you call a factory method that returns the appropriate object. Example: A LoggerFactory with createLogger() method. Based on configuration, it returns FileLogger, ConsoleLogger, or DatabaseLogger — all implementing a Logger interface. The client code only knows about Logger, not the specific implementations. Benefits: 1) Decouples object creation from usage. 2) Easy to add new types without changing client code. 3) Follows Open/Closed Principle. Used in: Java's Calendar.getInstance(), document editors (createDocument returns different doc types).

question: What is the 'this' pointer in C++? When is it used?
idealAnswer: 'this' is an implicit pointer available inside non-static member functions that points to the current object (the object on which the method was called). Uses: 1) Disambiguate member variables from parameters with the same name: this->x = x; 2) Return current object for method chaining: return *this; 3) Pass current object to another function: someFunction(this); 4) Compare object identity: if (this == &other). 'this' is a constant pointer — you can't change what it points to (this = something is invalid). Static methods don't have 'this' because they belong to the class, not an instance.

question: What is the diamond problem in multiple inheritance? How does C++ solve it?
idealAnswer: The diamond problem occurs when a class inherits from two classes that both inherit from a common base class. Example: class Animal; class Mammal : Animal; class WingedAnimal : Animal; class Bat : Mammal, WingedAnimal. Bat has TWO copies of Animal's members — ambiguity about which copy to use. C++ solves this with virtual inheritance: class Mammal : virtual public Animal; class WingedAnimal : virtual public Animal; Now Bat gets only ONE copy of Animal. The 'virtual' keyword ensures the common base class is shared, not duplicated. The most derived class (Bat) is responsible for constructing the virtual base class.
